item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wanted to say that all of this is great and it definitely deserves a +1, not a doubt in my mind.

I love both the concept and the narrative, beginning with how we were given the initial description, and then the narrative, besides interesting in all aspects, kept giving us more and more on what the scip is… And then you finish reading, and understand, and realize how creepy the concept itself is. Something that exists within itself, but wants out and can achieve so by feeding on belief? Wow…

And that's just about the narrative and the concept themselves. Then there's the "me" part. As an atheist and a mind "of science", I've had my fair share on thoughts on belief. Do I believe we evolved from bacteria? No. There's evidence supporting it, and so I think we did solely because of that. If enough evidence comes up and supports otherwise, I no longer will. Believing means to think something is as it is without tangible evidence…

But then, do I believe mankind can thrive? Be happy? Do I believe people can be good, can change? Does a foundation employee that's in it to make the world a better place believe they actually can achieve that? Oh, yes, we do! And this scip feeds on it! The thoughts that keep us going, the things making everything worth fighting for, are exactly on what it feeds!

The one thing that allows us to fight is the one thing that will lead us to fall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this one. The incident report at the end is what really sells it for me, I really felt that the whole world was falling apart, and I enjoy the creature's manipulation of faith. Thought it was a well done narrative their.

I am not sure about Prester John's Empire. The world building there just appears a bit strange and inconsistent. Maybe there is a whole lore there I am missing, but I don't really get why there are there are the conflicting three organizations. Or why the Order of the Flame wants to destroy Steve.

This is a really ambitious bold SCP. It doesn't all work, I thought the exploration logs were a bit weak, but I really enjoyed it overall.

item.title: Good Idea, but not quite my cup of tea
item.content:encoded:
Maybe this style of writing is not my type. I'm not a really good critic, but these are just my thoughts.

It's a good idea, but it wasn't executed well. This SCP is similar to "The Red Sea Object" SCP, but it's different enough for me. I like the idea of how the SCP uses people's faith to lure them and trick them into releasing it from the dimension it was trapped in.

Now for my dislikes. I don't really like how most of this SCP File is read as a story or a tale instead of an actual SCP File. The journal decryption is fine, but the security footage in the end and parts of the exploration didn't sound like normal human conversations, or things that a normal human would say; and were pretty dramatic. SCP Files (in my opinion. Feel free to object) are supposed to be documents composed of information relating to the SCP itself. Most of the unnecessary parts (anything that doesn't have to do with the SCP or anything relating to it) on the surveillance footage and exploration logs would've been cut off so there isn't too much to read

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sheesh. This one gave me the heebie jeebies at the end.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it! Reminds me a bit of 093, but different enough that it stands on its own.

+ But...
- just a note

I know this was meant to be a contest entry, but it didn't win. So probably you should change all the usages of 4000 to 4214.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made it through the first exploration log before calling it quits, given that I can see seven or so more collapsibles after that point, and taking into account the quality of the entry, I don't feel it's a worthy time investment to see it through 'till the end.

I will say that my primary issue is regarding the dialogue. The interview, while not abysmal, does not pull its weight in progressing the story, and is in fact a bit standard fare for someone suffering a compulsive effect. I feel as though it could be cleanly excised without sacrificing the integrity of the rest of the work.

Beyond that, the first audio log had a poor sense of time, making the narrative feel rushed, and not giving an in to really settle into the story world. Some audio cues, supplementary sounds and interjections to denote timegaps could help to alleviate this, but as it stands, it feels like it's moving far too quick for its own good, hampering immersion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be frank, I prefer SCP-3221's stab at Prester John over this.

Whereas that article has a unique spin on the CotBG, the Sarkic Cults, and GoIs in the face of a truly powerful awaiting force, here the world building is far too drawn out and the characters far too two dimensional, like mustache-twirling villains from old school cartoons.

On top of that, the exploration logs, which form the vast bulk of the article, are riddled with dialogue that doesn't read like actual people having a conversation. Dr. Blackburn is constantly pointing out the obvious and the D-class moves from robotic to swearing with little-maintained consistency.

-1. I can appreciate the attempt at world-building, but I am afraid it doesn't pan out here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing was cheesy at some points and jusr dismal in others (I can't say the little notes at the end of the logs were a good idea). But the imagery here is so vivid and the last log was so filled with tension that almost made up for it. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some SCPs I can see clearly in my mind as I read them. This definitely has that quality. There was a definite sense that everything was going to go pear-shaped, and when it did I was not disappointed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its well written but abit too cheesy for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Carbon dating indicates that the archway is roughly 900 years old

Carbon dating only works on things that are made of carbon. This archway is made of sandstone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably too dramatic and cinematic in the way it's structured, but I still like it enough to upvote despite having some reservations about dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate saying this, but I kind of don't feel like "atheist guards" is proof enough against something linked to religion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me personally, this felt like a lot to get through for not much payoff. There's also quite a bit of unnatural feeling dialogue. I feel like the concept might have some potential, but execution needs a lot of work.

-1
